title: Car Components
subtitle: Click the red hotspots to learn about each part.

hotspots.name: Front Wing
hotspots.desc: Generates downforce at the nose. Small angle tweaks shift the handling balance — teams adjust it lap by lap via radio to counter tire wear.

hotspots.name: Front Tires
hotspots.desc: Pirelli-supplied slicks. The fronts handle braking and turn-in loads, and run cooler than the rears — making them less prone to graining.

hotspots.name: Halo
hotspots.desc: Mandatory titanium safety arch above the cockpit. Protects the driver from debris and rollover forces. Introduced in 2018 and has since saved multiple lives.

hotspots.name: Sidepod
hotspots.desc: Houses the water and oil radiators. The shape is among the most contested aerodynamic battlegrounds — teams spend millions optimizing the airflow around it.

hotspots.name: Rear Tires
hotspots.desc: Wider than the fronts to handle power delivery. Most vulnerable to degradation under heavy acceleration — often the deciding factor in pit strategy.

hotspots.name: Rear Wing
hotspots.desc: The main downforce generator. DRS opens the rear flap on designated straights, cutting drag and increasing top speed by up to 15 km/h.

title: Tire Compounds
subtitle: F1 uses color-coded compounds. Click one to learn its purpose.

tires.name: Soft
tires.text: SOFT — Fastest compound on track, generating maximum grip. Typically lasts only 10–15 laps before the cliff. Used in qualifying and attack stints.

tires.name: Medium
tires.text: MEDIUM — The race workhorse. Balanced grip and durability, usually good for 20–30 laps. Most strategies are built around it.

tires.name: Hard
tires.text: HARD — Slowest but can run 40+ laps. Preferred for long stints or when track temps are extreme. Less sensitive to thermal degradation.

tires.name: Inter
tires.text: INTERMEDIATE — Built for damp or drying conditions. Deep tread evacuates water without full wet grooves. Often the fastest option in tricky weather transitions.

tires.name: Full Wet
tires.text: FULL WET — For heavy rain. Channels up to 85 litres of water per second per tire. Teams move to slicks as soon as conditions allow.

title: Tire Degradation
subtitle: Drag the slider to see how tire wear affects lap time.

title: Performance Degradation
subtitle: Drag to see how grip level changes lap speed.

title: Aerodynamics
subtitle: Why air turbulence is one of the biggest weapons in F1 strategy.

cards.label: Clean Air
cards.desc: Running in open track, your car cuts through smooth, undisturbed air. Downforce is maximized, cornering speeds are higher, and tires behave predictably.

cards.label: Dirty Air
cards.desc: Following another car closely puts you in its turbulent wake. Downforce drops, corners get slower, and tires slide and overheat — erasing any speed advantage from new rubber.

note: Teams must decide: pit early for fresh tires and race in clean air — or stay out and hope the car ahead pits first. Dirty air can completely negate a tire compound advantage, making track position as important as pace.

title: Finding the Gap
subtitle: Select the best gap to pit into. You want clean air and track position.

gaps.label: TOO SMALL
gaps.feedback: ✗ Too tight — you'd emerge right behind that car and lose all your fresh tire advantage in dirty air.

gaps.label: Click to pit here
gaps.feedback: ✓ Perfect call! This gap gives enough clean air and track position after the pit stop.

gaps.label: Click to pit here
gaps.feedback: ⚠ Possible, but you'd lap behind a backmarker. Gap B gives cleaner air and stronger track position.
